{{birth date|1630|12|17}}|death_date={{death date|1714|10|5}}; {{death date|1714|7|27}}|birth_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|death_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|botanist|WD=Q2374149}}; {{ill|philosopher|WD=Q4964182}}|spouse={{ill|Kaibara Tōken|WD=Q121009857}}|notable_works={{ill|Chikuzen'nokuni Zoku Fudoki|WD=Q108571667}}; {{ill|Q11417150|WD=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|WD=Q65294057}}; {{ill|Yojokun|WD=Q11666861}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|WD=Q11433474}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|WD=Q9581}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1721396]] Wikidata name: Kaibara Ekken [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1721396]] Wikidata name: Kaibara Ekken [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese [[:en:Neo-Confucianism|Neo-Confucianist]] [[:en:Philosopher|Philosopher]] and [[:en:Botanist|Botanist]].
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] in [[:en:Chikuzen Province|Chikuzen Province]] (modern-day [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]). He accompanied his father to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to [[:en:Nagasaki|Nagasaki]] to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a [[:en:Rōnin|Rōnin]] from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], and reflect similar concerns to the [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the [[:en:National Museum of Nature and Science|National Museum of Nature and Science]], Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to [[:en:Botany|Botany]] and [[:en:Materia medica|Materia medica]] and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as [[:en:Charles Darwin|Charles Darwin]] when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist [[:en:Philipp Franz von Siebold|Philipp Franz von Siebold]] called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of [[:en:Zhu Xi|Zhu Xi]] (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

London: John Murray. * [[:en:Dorothy Ko|Ko, Dorothy]], [[:en:JaHyun Kim Haboush|JaHyun Kim Haboush]] and [[:en:Joan R. Piggott|Joan R. Piggott]]. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' Berkeley: [[:en:University of California Press|University of California Press]]. {{ISBN|0-520-23138-4}} * Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|0-520-23269-0}}

Yoshino, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1714}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080309203119/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/itsukushima_e.html Itsukushima kakei,'' guide to Itsukushima, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1720}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080403190545/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/tanngonokuni_e.html ''Tanngo no kuni Amano hashidate no zu,'' guide to Ama-no-Hashidate, text by Eiken ({{Circa|1726}})] * [https://kotenseki.nijl.ac.jp/app/l/?ln=en#%2Fsl%2Fsi%2F%7B%22keyword%22:%22%E8%B2%9D%E5%8E%9F%20%E7%9B%8A%E8%BB%92%22,%22sortField%22:%22s.wid%22,%22sortOrder%22:%22acs%22,%22sz%22:20,%22fr%22:0%7D "Database of Pre-Modern Japanese Works"] (National Institute of Japanese Literature): Books by Kaibara Ekiken

''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>

Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

{{birth date|1630|12|17}}|death_date={{death date|1714|10|5}}; {{death date|1714|7|27}}|birth_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|death_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|botanist|WD=Q2374149}}; {{ill|philosopher|WD=Q4964182}}|spouse={{ill|Kaibara Tōken|WD=Q121009857}}|notable_works={{ill|Chikuzen'nokuni Zoku Fudoki|WD=Q108571667}}; {{ill|Q11417150|WD=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|WD=Q65294057}}; {{ill|Yojokun|WD=Q11666861}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|WD=Q11433474}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|WD=Q9581}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1721396]] Wikidata name: Kaibara Ekken [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1721396]] Wikidata name: Kaibara Ekken [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|WD=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|WD=Q2374149|lt=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>
==See also==

London: John Murray. * [[:en:Dorothy Ko|Ko, Dorothy]], {{ill|JaHyun Kim Haboush|en|JaHyun Kim Haboush|WD=Q16972736|lt=JaHyun Kim Haboush}} and {{ill|Joan R. Piggott|en|Joan R. Piggott|WD=Q111551261|lt=Joan R. Piggott}}. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|0-520-23138-4}} * Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|0-520-23269-0}}

Yoshino, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1714}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080309203119/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/itsukushima_e.html Itsukushima kakei,'' guide to Itsukushima, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1720}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080403190545/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/tanngonokuni_e.html ''Tanngo no kuni Amano hashidate no zu,'' guide to Ama-no-Hashidate, text by Eiken ({{Circa|1726}})] * [https://kotenseki.nijl.ac.jp/app/l/?ln=en#%2Fsl%2Fsi%2F%7B%22keyword%22:%22%E8%B2%9D%E5%8E%9F%20%E7%9B%8A%E8%BB%92%22,%22sortField%22:%22s.wid%22,%22sortOrder%22:%22acs%22,%22sz%22:20,%22fr%22:0%7D "Database of Pre-Modern Japanese Works"] (National Institute of Japanese Literature): Books by Kaibara Ekiken

Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|WD=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|WD=Q2374149|lt=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

London: John Murray. * [[:en:Dorothy Ko|Ko, Dorothy]], {{ill|JaHyun Kim Haboush|en|JaHyun Kim Haboush|WD=Q16972736|lt=JaHyun Kim Haboush}} and {{ill|Joan R. Piggott|en|Joan R. Piggott|WD=Q111551261|lt=Joan R. Piggott}}. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|0-520-23138-4}} * Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). Yoshino, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1714}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080309203119/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/itsukushima_e.html Itsukushima kakei,'' guide to Itsukushima, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1720}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080403190545/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/tanngonokuni_e.html ''Tanngo no kuni Amano hashidate no zu,'' guide to Ama-no-Hashidate, text by Eiken ({{Circa|1726}})] * [https://kotenseki.nijl.ac.jp/app/l/?ln=en#%2Fsl%2Fsi%2F%7B%22keyword%22:%22%E8%B2%9D%E5%8E%9F%20%E7%9B%8A%E8%BB%92%22,%22sortField%22:%22s.wid%22,%22sortOrder%22:%22acs%22,%22sz%22:20,%22fr%22:0%7D "Database of Pre-Modern Japanese Works"] (National Institute of Japanese Literature): Books by Kaibara Ekiken

Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ekiken Kaibara]][[de:Kaibara Ekiken]][[en:Kaibara Ekken]][[es:Kaibara Ekiken]][[fa:کایبارا اککن]][[fr:Kaibara Ekken]][[it:Ekken Kaibara]][[ja:貝原益軒]][[ko:가이바라 에키켄]][[pl:Ekken Kaibara]][[ru:Кайбара Эккэн]][[sl:Kaibara Ekken]][[th:เอกิเก็ง ไคบาระ]][[vi:Kaibara Ekiken]][[zh:贝原益轩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ekiken Kaibara]][[de:Kaibara Ekiken]][[en:Kaibara Ekken]][[es:Kaibara Ekiken]][[fa:کایبارا اککن]][[fr:Kaibara Ekken]][[it:Ekken Kaibara]][[ja:貝原益軒]][[ko:가이바라 에키켄]][[pl:Ekken Kaibara]][[ru:Кайбара Эккэн]][[sl:Kaibara Ekken]][[th:เอกิเก็ง ไคบาระ]][[vi:Kaibara Ekiken]][[zh:贝原益轩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ekiken Kaibara]][[de:Kaibara Ekiken]][[en:Kaibara Ekken]][[es:Kaibara Ekiken]][[fa:کایبارا اککن]][[fr:Kaibara Ekken]][[it:Ekken Kaibara]][[ja:貝原益軒]][[ko:가이바라 에키켄]][[pl:Ekken Kaibara]][[ru:Кайбара Эккэн]][[sl:Kaibara Ekken]][[th:เอกิเก็ง ไคบาระ]][[vi:Kaibara Ekiken]][[zh:贝原益轩]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Ekiken Kaibara]][[de:Kaibara Ekiken]][[en:Kaibara Ekken]][[es:Kaibara Ekiken]][[fa:کایبارا اککن]][[fr:Kaibara Ekken]][[it:Ekken Kaibara]][[ja:貝原益軒]][[ko:가이바라 에키켄]][[pl:Ekken Kaibara]][[ru:Кайбара Эккэн]][[sl:Kaibara Ekken]][[th:เอกิเก็ง ไคบาระ]][[vi:Kaibara Ekiken]][[zh:贝原益轩]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Portrait-Kaibara-Ekiken-(cropped)-c1700.png|birth_date={{birth date|1630|10|14}}; {{birth date|1630|12|17}}|death_date={{death date|1714|10|5}}; {{death date|1714|7|27}}|birth_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|death_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|botanist|WD=Q2374149}}; {{ill|philosopher|WD=Q4964182}}|spouse={{ill|Kaibara Tōken|WD=Q121009857}}|notable_works={{ill|Chikuzen'nokuni Zoku Fudoki|WD=Q108571667}}; {{ill|Q11417150|WD=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|WD=Q65294057}}; {{ill|Yojokun|WD=Q11666861}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|WD=Q11433474}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|WD=Q9581}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}
{{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|WD=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|WD=Q2374149|lt=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>
==See also==
* [[Confucian Shinto]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|2}}
==References== * Kaibara, Ekiken and Shingoro Takaishi. (1905). '''' (Basil Hall Chamberlain, translator). London: John Murray. ** Cranmer-Byng, L. and S.A. Kapadia, eds. (1914). ''''. London: John Murray. * [[:en:Dorothy Ko|Ko, Dorothy]], {{ill|JaHyun Kim Haboush|en|JaHyun Kim Haboush|WD=Q16972736|lt=JaHyun Kim Haboush}} and {{ill|Joan R. Piggott|en|Joan R. Piggott|WD=Q111551261|lt=Joan R. Piggott}}. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|0-520-23138-4}} * Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|0-520-23269-0}}

Yoshino, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1714}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080309203119/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/itsukushima_e.html Itsukushima kakei,'' guide to Itsukushima, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1720}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080403190545/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/tanngonokuni_e.html ''Tanngo no kuni Amano hashidate no zu,'' guide to Ama-no-Hashidate, text by Eiken ({{Circa|1726}})] * [https://kotenseki.nijl.ac.jp/app/l/?ln=en#%2Fsl%2Fsi%2F%7B%22keyword%22:%22%E8%B2%9D%E5%8E%9F%20%E7%9B%8A%E8%BB%92%22,%22sortField%22:%22s.wid%22,%22sortOrder%22:%22acs%22,%22sz%22:20,%22fr%22:0%7D "Database of Pre-Modern Japanese Works"] (National Institute of Japanese Literature): Books by Kaibara Ekiken

[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science|1=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

{{birth date|1630|12|17}}|death_date={{death date|1714|10|5}}; {{death date|1714|7|27}}|birth_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}|death_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|botanist|WD=Q2374149|1=Botanist}}; {{ill|philosopher|WD=Q4964182|1=Philosopher}}|spouse={{ill|Kaibara Tōken|WD=Q121009857|1=Kaibara Tōken}}|notable_works={{ill|Chikuzen'nokuni Zoku Fudoki|WD=Q108571667|1=Chikuzen no Kuni Zoku Fudoki}}; {{ill|Q11417150|WD=Q11417150|1=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|WD=Q65294057|1=Kuroda Family Genealogy}}; {{ill|Yojokun|WD=Q11666861|1=Yojokun}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|WD=Q11433474|1=yamatohonzō}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|1=Confucianism}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}
{{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist|1=Neo-Confucianism}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|WD=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher|1=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|WD=Q2374149|lt=Botanist|1=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain|1=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin|1=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science|1=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany|1=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica|1=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin|1=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

{{ill|Q11417150|WD=Q11417150|1=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|WD=Q65294057|1=Kuroda Family Genealogy}}; {{ill|Yojokun|WD=Q11666861|1=Yojokun}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|WD=Q11433474|1=yamatohonzō}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|1=Confucianism}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}
{{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|WD=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist|1=Neo-Confucianism}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|WD=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher|1=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|WD=Q2374149|lt=Botanist|1=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain|1=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|WD=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|WD=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|WD=Q212418|lt=Rōnin|1=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|WD=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science|1=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|WD=Q441|lt=Botany|1=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|WD=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica|1=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|WD=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin|1=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|WD=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|WD=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>
==See also==
* [[Confucian Shinto]]

London: John Murray. * [[:en:Dorothy Ko|Ko, Dorothy]], {{ill|JaHyun Kim Haboush|en|JaHyun Kim Haboush|WD=Q16972736|lt=JaHyun Kim Haboush|1=JaHyun Kim Haboush}} and {{ill|Joan R. Piggott|en|Joan R. Piggott|WD=Q111551261|lt=Joan R. Piggott|1=Joan R. Piggott}}. (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press|1=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|0-520-23138-4}} * Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|0-520-23269-0}}

{{Infobox person|image=File:Portrait-Kaibara-Ekiken-(cropped)-c1700.png|birth_date={{birth date|1630|10|14}}; {{birth date|1630|12|17}}|death_date={{death date|1714|10|5}}; {{death date|1714|7|27}}|birth_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}|death_place={{ill|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|botanist|qid=Q2374149|1=Botanist}}; {{ill|philosopher|qid=Q4964182|1=Philosopher}}|spouse={{ill|Kaibara Tōken|qid=Q121009857|1=Kaibara Tōken}}|notable_works={{ill|Chikuzen'nokuni Zoku Fudoki|qid=Q108571667|1=Chikuzen no Kuni Zoku Fudoki}}; {{ill|Q11417150|qid=Q11417150|1=Q11417150}}; {{ill|Q65294057|qid=Q65294057|1=Kuroda Family Genealogy}}; {{ill|Yojokun|qid=Q11666861|1=Yojokun}}; {{ill|yamatohonzō|qid=Q11433474|1=yamatohonzō}}|religion={{ill|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|1=Confucianism}}|native_name=貝原益軒}}
{{Short description|Japanese Confucianist Philosopher, Pre-Linnaean botanist}} [[File:Kaibara Ekiken monument.jpg|thumb|250px| Bronze Statue of Kaibara Ekken at his gravesite (Kinryū-Temple, Fukuoka-City, Japan)]] __NOTOC__ {{family name hatnote|Kaibara|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Kaibara Ekken'''|貝原 益軒||December 17, 1630 – October 5, 1714}} or '''Ekiken''', also known as '''Atsunobu''' (篤信), was a Japanese {{ill|Neo-Confucianism|en|Neo-Confucianism|qid=Q825754|lt=Neo-Confucianist|1=Neo-Confucianism}} {{ill|Philosopher|en|Philosopher|qid=Q4964182|lt=Philosopher|1=Philosopher}} and {{ill|Botanist|en|Botanist|qid=Q2374149|lt=Botanist|1=Botanist}}.
Kaibara was born into a family of advisors to the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|qid=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain|1=Fukuoka Domain}} in {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen Province|1=Chikuzen Province}} (modern-day {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}). He accompanied his father to {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in 1648, and was sent in 1649 to {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}} to study Western science. At his father's urging, he continued his studies in Nagasaki as a {{ill|Rōnin|en|Rōnin|qid=Q212418|lt=Rōnin|1=Rōnin}} from 1650 through 1656. He then re-entered service to Kuroda, which led to his continuing studies in Kyoto. After his father's death in 1665, he returned to Fukuoka.<ref name="y49">Yonemoto, Marcia. (2003). ''Mapping Early Modern Japan: Space, Place, and Culture in the Tokugawa Period (1603–1868).'' p. 49.</ref>
Kaibara's two most significant contributions to Japanese culture were the study of nature based on a blend of Western natural science and Neo-Confucianism, and the translation of the complex writings of Neo-Confucianism into vernacular Japanese. His synthesis of Confucian ideas and Western science influence the formation of [[Shinto]], especially {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, and reflect similar concerns to the {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} movement.<ref>{{cite book|first=Jason Ā. |last=Josephson|title=The Invention of Religion in Japan|location=Chicago|publisher=University of Chicago Press|year=2012|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gxzuzIszO60C|pages=115–6|isbn=9780226412351}}</ref>
[[File:Yamato Honzo.jpg|thumb|right|''Yamato honzō''. Book of botany written by Kaibara Ekken in 1709. Exhibit in the {{ill|National Museum of Nature and Science|en|National Museum of Nature and Science|qid=Q74940|lt=National Museum of Nature and Science|1=National Museum of Nature and Science}}, Tokyo, Japan.]] [[File:Youjyoukun.jpg|thumb|right|''Yōjōkun''. Book of healthy way written by Kaibara Ekken in 1713.]] Kaibara's science was confined to {{ill|Botany|en|Botany|qid=Q441|lt=Botany|1=Botany}} and {{ill|Materia medica|en|Materia medica|qid=Q1291275|lt=Materia medica|1=Materia medica}} and focused on the "natural law". Kaibara became as famous in Japan as people such as {{ill|Charles Darwin|en|Charles Darwin|qid=Q1035|lt=Charles Darwin|1=Charles Darwin}} when it came to science. He advanced the study of botany in Japan when he wrote ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), which was a seminal study of Japanese [[:en:plants|plants]]. The 19th-century German Japanologist {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|en|Philipp Franz von Siebold|qid=Q77140|lt=Philipp Franz von Siebold|1=Philipp Franz von Siebold}} called him the "Aristotle of Japan".<ref name="y49" />
Kaibara was known for his manuals of behavior, such as changing his Confucian ethical system based on the teachings of {{ill|Zhu Xi|en|Zhu Xi|qid=Q9397|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Zhu Xi}} (also known as Chu Hsi) into an easy "self-help" manuals. As an educator and philosopher, it appears that Kaibara's main goal in life was to further the process of weaving Neo-Confucianism into Japanese culture. In this context, he is best known for such books as ''Precepts for Children'' and ''Greater Learning for Women'' (''Onna daigaku''); but modern scholarship argues that it was actually prepared by other hands. Although the genesis of the work remains unchallenged, the oldest extant copy (1733) ends with the lines "as related by our teacher Ekiken Kaibara" and the publisher's colophon states that the text was written from lectures of our teacher Kaibara."<ref>Ko, Dorothy ''et al.'' (2003). ''Women and Confucian Cultures in Premodern China, Korea, and Japan.'' p. 199.</ref>
==Published works== * ''Dazaifu jinja engi'' (History of Dazaifu Shrine).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=468 Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Personalities : Kaibara Ekiken]</ref> * ''Jingikun'' (Lessons of the Deities).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Onna daigaku'' (Greater Learning for Women), {{Circa|1729}}.<ref>[http://chnm.gmu.edu/wwh/p/84.html See excerpt] (hosted by George Mason University)</ref> * ''Shinju heikō aimotorazaru ron'' (Treatise on the Non-Divergence of Shintō and Confucianism).<ref name="eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp"/> * ''Yamato honzō'' (Medicinal herbs of Japan), 1709. * ''Yamato sōhon'' (Grasses of Japan).<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online">{{Cite web |url=http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |title=Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online |access-date=2008-01-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120207133539/http://www.iias.nl/iiasn/16/regions/ea4.html |archive-date=2012-02-07 |url-status=dead }}</ref> * ''Yōjōkun'' (The Book of Life-nourishing Principles), 1713.<ref name="Regions - IIAS Newsletter Online"/> * ''Taigiroku'' (The Record of Great Doubts), posthumously published in 1714.<ref>{{cite book |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition |volume=1 |edition=2nd |isbn=9780231121385 |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor1-last=de Bary |editor2-first=Donald |editor2-last=Keene |display-editors=et al |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2002}}{{page needed|date=October 2021}}</ref>

Yoshino, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1714}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080309203119/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/itsukushima_e.html Itsukushima kakei,'' guide to Itsukushima, text by Ekiken ({{Circa|1720}})] ** [https://web.archive.org/web/20080403190545/http://jpimg.digital.archives.go.jp/kouseisai/Category/emaki/tanngonokuni_e.html ''Tanngo no kuni Amano hashidate no zu,'' guide to Ama-no-Hashidate, text by Eiken ({{Circa|1726}})] * [https://kotenseki.nijl.ac.jp/app/l/?ln=en#%2Fsl%2Fsi%2F%7B%22keyword%22:%22%E8%B2%9D%E5%8E%9F%20%E7%9B%8A%E8%BB%92%22,%22sortField%22:%22s.wid%22,%22sortOrder%22:%22acs%22,%22sz%22:20,%22fr%22:0%7D "Database of Pre-Modern Japanese Works"] (National Institute of Japanese Literature): Books by Kaibara Ekiken
